name: Phase 2 — Sidequest: The Sign of the Brown Hare

pages.name: GM Brief — What This Sidequest Is

text.content:
The Sign of the Brown Hare — GM Brief

An optional Phase 2 sidequest. Triggered by a single hand-drawn mark — “R.” next to a roadhouse south of Loftwick — on the third map in the brass tube of Haskur's recovered possessions (see Phase 1: Caelith Dunivar, Evidence Item 6, The Tube of Maps). The mark matches Haskur's own spellbook hand at Investigation DC 14.

The one sentence

Haskur Vandrell has a half-sister who runs a small roadhouse two days' ride south of Loftwick, and she has watched him become a stranger to her over thirty years.

Why this sidequest exists

This is a red herring on its face and a relief beat in practice. Mechanically it carries no new conspiracy intelligence. The party will not learn anything operational about Cindren & Vhal, the “E.” hand, the under-road, or the harbor commission play. They will lose five days off the main spine and advance the Pressure Clock by two segments for their trouble.

What the sidequest gives them instead is the woman who watched the man they fought stop being her brother. Rosalin Brent — mid-fifties, capable, weathered, careful — has seen Haskur eight times in nine years and has not received a letter from him in fourteen months. She knows nothing about Andren, the firm, or the eastern road. She knows everything about a boy who came to her at fourteen, after their mother died, and left at sixteen wearing other people's coats, and never quite came home again.

Her thesis — delivered late, after the party has eaten her bread and proved they did not ride south to kill her brother — is the one line Phase 2 needs to land in a register Caelith cannot supply:

“Hask was never clever. He was hungry and he was beautiful and he was furious, and one of those is enough on its own to ruin a man and he had all three. Someone with a head on their shoulders found him and put work in his hands, and that work is what is wearing his face now. He is not the one writing the letters. He never could have been. Look for the hand that does the writing. That is the man who took him from me.”

It is Caelith's framing — “He is the man someone made, the man someone keeps making” — delivered as a sister's grief instead of a spymaster's analysis. The same truth, in a register the party cannot brush off.

What it is NOT

  • Not a combat sidequest. The optional bandit encounter on the road south is three Yeomanry deserters; talk-down DC 12. If the party kills them, they kill three poor men with no conspiracy connection.
  • Not a clue. Nothing the party learns at the Brown Hare is actionable against C&V, the firm in Hardby, the senior partners in Rel Astra, or anyone else.
  • Not a lead to a hidden cache. The cedar box in Rosalin's upstairs storage contains a fishing knife, a child's prayer-book, and a wooden horse with a glued-on head. No gold. No paper. No conspiracy.
  • Not a hostage situation in the making. Rosalin is not being watched. The firm does not know she exists. Do not connect her to the conspiracy as a clean-up target in any later phase — that would betray what the sidequest is for.

What it IS

  • The party's first concrete moment of grief on this campaign. Phase 1 was a hero's welcome. Phase 2 is administrative dread. This is the relief beat between those two registers — a quiet day at a country inn, a woman pouring tea, a brother's childhood things in a cedar box.
  • Validation of what the party is starting to suspect — that Haskur is not the conspiracy's hand but its instrument — delivered without operational detail. They leave with conviction, not facts.
  • A character moment for whichever PC takes Rosalin's letter. She has written a letter to her brother she has never sent. If the party asks for it, she gives it to them under one condition: if Hask is ever in a room they leave alive, they put it in front of him and do not say her name. Whichever PC carries that letter into Phase 6 (where Haskur is recovered as Andren at the Brass Crow) has a personal scene to play.

Pacing

In-fiction daysWhat happensPressure Clock
Day 1 (down)The party rides south on the Wool Road. Optional bandit encounter at the Stell crossing late afternoon (see The Ride South).+0 during travel; +1 at end of day if Wick Lane / Tannerway threads are open and unworked.
Day 2 (down)Arrival at the Brown Hare in the late afternoon. Dinner. Introductions. Rosalin pours tea. She does not yet know why they are there.+0 (off the conspiracy's view)
Day 3 (at the Hare)Morning conversations. Hask's box. The letter. Rosalin's thesis. They leave in the late afternoon for the inn at Stell.+0
Day 4 (back)Ride north.+1 (Korre's watcher in Loftwick has lost them and reports up).
Day 5 (back)Arrival in Loftwick at midday. Caelith does not ask where they were.+1 (broadsheet rumour begins; the matter is starting to be on the streets).

Net cost: 5 in-fiction days off the spine; +2 Pressure Clock segments overall (one each on the return ride). If the party is already at clock 3+ when they consider this sidequest, advise them in Caelith's voice: “You may be back to find the door has shut on most of what you were chasing.”

Recommended trigger

Players who find the “R.” mark on the third map (Investigation DC 14) and ask Caelith about it. His honest answer:

“I do not know what it is. The hand is his — not the other hand we have learned to look for. South of Loftwick, on the Wool Road, two days' ride. There is a roadhouse there called the Sign of the Brown Hare. The proprietor's name is on no Yeomanry register I have looked at, which is unusual but not damning — many rural inns are run by people whose names belong to no register. If you ride south to look at it, you will not learn what we are looking for. You may learn something else. Decide whether the cost is acceptable.”

If the party never finds the mark, never asks about it, or asks and declines — this sidequest never happens. That is fine. Nothing downstream depends on it.

The Ride South — Day 1 to the Brown Hare

Read aloud at the South Gate

The Wool Road south is broad and pale and almost empty at this hour. You have not taken it before. The road you came home on three nights ago ran north out of the Jotuns; the road east, which you will take in three days under whichever cover you choose, runs out of the city through the Civic Way and onto a country lane lined with poplars. This is neither of those roads. This is the road that takes drovers and grain-carts and the occasional Yeomanry militia patrol to half a dozen small villages scattered through the southern reach of the Sheldomar drainage — thin farms, a few wool-houses, three or four crossroads inns where you can get a hot meal and a bed for a copper.

The sun is high. The fields beside the road are barley and oats, mostly oats; the hedgerows are still in their summer fullness; somewhere off to the southwest, a sheep is complaining about something to nobody. You can ride two abreast comfortably and three if you crowd. By the second bell after noon you have passed the milestone marked STELL XI — eleven Yeomanry miles to the village of Stell, where the small two-storey inn called the Plough is the only commercial premises — and the road has begun to bend south-by-southwest, following the slow curve of the river.

You will reach Stell by sundown if you do not push the horses. You will reach the Brown Hare, six miles past Stell on a smaller side-track, by midmorning tomorrow.

Loftwick is behind you. The conspiracy is behind you. For this one afternoon, nobody is reading you.

The country between Loftwick and Stell

The Wool Road runs south through farming country that is, in 581 CY, almost entirely Yeomanry-aligned and quiet. The party will pass:

  • Three small farmsteads with sheep cotes; a farm-wife at one of them sells them ale and brown bread for two coppers if they ask.
  • A Yeomanry militia signal-post (unmanned at this hour). A small whitewashed cairn marks it. Investigation DC 10 reads the militia roster posted under glass — standard quarterly rotation.
  • The Stell crossing — a wooden bridge over a slow tributary of the Sheldomar, locally called the Stellwater. A small stone toll-house at the south end, manned by an elderly Yeomanry pensioner named Old Beck, who charges nothing and waves the party through with a salute when he sees their gear is military-grade and their bearing is military-trained.

Optional encounter — the Stell-crossing deserters

Trigger: GM choice. Skip entirely if the table has had enough combat in Phase 1 and Phase 2 already, or if the party is small / under-rested.

About a mile past the Stell crossing, in a small wood beside the road, the party catches the smell of a cookfire that has been hastily smothered. Three men step into the road behind them with bows half-drawn. They are not threatening exactly — they are tired, hungry, and embarrassed.

Who they are

Three Yeomanry militiamen who were under recruitment for the eastern war scare last winter, were promised back-pay that did not arrive in full, and after the scare passed quietly deserted rather than wait out the bureaucracy. Their names are Tomm Loar (a former farrier, the leader), Andren Wisk (no relation to Haskur's alias; bad luck for the GM), and young Joren (sixteen, sleeping in a coat too big for him).

What they want

Hot food, a few coppers, and not to be reported to the Yeomanry watch. They will not shoot unless attacked. Tomm Loar speaks first; his words come out half-rehearsed and half-apologetic:

“Friends — we are not robbers. We are hungry men who did not get paid what we were owed. We saw your horses and we hoped you might spare us a few coins or a hot meal between you. We will not hurt you. We will not even ask your business. If you say no, we will step aside.”

Resolution options

  • Persuasion DC 12 or sharing trail rations: the three men accept gratefully, eat with the party, and tell them which side-road forks off the Wool Road for the Brown Hare (one less Survival check needed tomorrow). They warn the party that Rosalin Brent at the Hare “does not like soldiers in her common room but she is courteous to travelers,” which is the first the party hears Rosalin's name.
  • Intimidation DC 10: the three back off and slip into the wood. No conflict. No information gained.
  • Combat: three Bandit statblocks (MM 2024 Bandit; CR 1/8 each). Tomm fights with a longbow at range, Andren and Joren with shortswords. They surrender at half HP or when Joren falls. If the party kills any of them, the GM should note this quietly — nothing mechanical, but Rosalin will read it on their faces at dinner the next night, and she will be cooler to them than she would otherwise have been.
  • Reporting them to Old Beck on the way back is also possible. Beck will note the description with regret and not pursue. The Yeomanry's response to small-scale wartime desertion in 581 CY is restrained.

This encounter has zero connection to the main conspiracy. The deserters are exactly what they appear to be. The GM may use the encounter to test whether the party is in “administrative dread” mode or in “combat clearing” mode; the difference will read on Rosalin's face at dinner.

Stell & the Plough — overnight

The party reaches Stell by sundown. The Plough is a plain two-storey inn run by Mistress Maela Cosk, a heavyset widow in her sixties who has eight grandchildren and remembers everyone she has ever served. She does not know Rosalin Brent personally but knows the Sign of the Brown Hare by name and gives directions: “Six miles south, then west at the second fork where the standing-stone sits. The Hare is signposted; you'll see the carved animal.”

Beds at the Plough: 2 sp per bed. Stable: 1 sp per horse. Mistress Cosk's lamb stew is famous within twenty miles and excellent. She will accept a Yeomanry trade slug as payment, which the party may or may not have on them.

The party should arrive at the Brown Hare by midmorning the following day.

pages.name: The Sign of the Brown Hare (Keyed)

text.content:
The Sign of the Brown Hare

Arrival read-aloud

You come around the bend of the side-track at midmorning and the roadhouse is the first thing you see: a low timber-and-stone building with a slate roof and dark wooden corner posts, set well back from the road in a small yard with a stable and a woodshed at its back. The sign above the door is the carved figure of a brown hare in mid-leap, painted years ago and weathered to a softer brown than the wood it was carved from. A small stone path leads to the door. A wash-line is up in the yard behind the house with three white aprons drying in the morning sun.

A woman comes around the side of the building before you have dismounted. She is in her mid-fifties, plain forest-green dress under a flour-dusted apron, her hair pulled back in a careful bun. She is carrying a wicker basket of new-laid eggs against her hip. She stops where she stands when she sees you, and the basket steadies very slightly against her hip.

For one half-second her face does something you cannot quite read.

Then it is gone, and she is just a country inn-keeper meeting six strangers at her door.

“Good morning,” she says. Her voice is low and level. “Are you riding through or are you stopping?”

GM note: the half-second is real. Rosalin recognizes whichever of the party most resembles her brother — either by build, by bearing, or by a small detail that catches her eye (a particular kind of confidence, a hand-shape, the way someone tilts their head). She does not say so. She does not ever, in this entire sidequest, say so aloud.

The roadhouse, in keyed locations

The map in assets/maps/brown-hare-roadhouse-interior.png shows both floors stacked vertically. Refer to the Foundry scene Brown Hare — Roadhouse for the walls/doors/lights pass.

Ground floor

G1 — Common Room (front)

The largest room of the inn; four heavy oak tables with mismatched stools, a large stone fireplace on the west wall (lit even in summer, low; the cooking-iron above it holds a slow-cooking cauldron of barley stew), wooden benches along the east wall under two leaded-glass windows that overlook the road. A faded red rag-rug runs from the front door to the bar. The room smells of wood-smoke, fresh bread, and a faint sweetness from a posy of dried lavender hanging by the fireplace.

On a normal day: two or three local farmers eating a midday meal, an old shepherd named Old Pell who sits in the same corner every afternoon and never says anything, occasionally a Yeomanry militia patrol on rotation from the signal-post at Reedrush.

On the day the party arrives: Old Pell is in his corner. Nobody else.

G2 — Bar Counter

Polished plank counter along the north wall, a row of pewter mugs hanging behind, two small wooden casks of ale on stands (a thin local barley ale at 4 cp per pint; a heavier amber ale at 6 cp per pint that Rosalin brews herself). A short flap section opens for staff to pass through. Behind the bar, on a small shelf, a Heironeous wayfarer's medallion in plain pewter, dusty.

G3 — Kitchen

Brick hearth-oven on the south wall (separated from the common-room fireplace by a stone chimney shared between them; the fireplace warms the common room, the oven cooks the food). A sturdy worktable in the centre. A chopping block. A stone sink with a hand-pump. Hanging copper pots. A back door in the east wall leads out to the yard.

G4 — Scullery / Pantry

Annex to the kitchen; shelves of dry goods, jars of pickled vegetables and preserved fruit (Rosalin's own; the cherry preserves are famous within ten miles), three hanging cured hams, sacks of flour, a stand of crockery, a small lockbox in a high shelf that contains the inn's daily takings (typically 8–15 gp at any given time; Rosalin moves it to her chambers each evening).

G5 — Back Yard, Stable & Woodshed

A small walled yard about 30 ft by 20 ft. Three stable stalls with straw bedding (Rosalin's own horse Burdock — an elderly grey gelding — lives in the first; the other two are available for guests). A wood-shed stacked with split firewood. A stone well with a wooden cover at the centre of the yard. The wash-line. A small kitchen garden along the east wall with herbs, a few rows of beans, and three rosemary bushes that Rosalin planted twenty years ago and refuses to thin.

Upper floor

U1 — Stair Landing

Top of the narrow wooden stair, west end of the upper floor. Two oil lamps in wall sconces.

U2 — Guest Room One (west)

A single neatly-made narrow bed, a small wash-stand with porcelain pitcher and basin, a small wardrobe, a single south-facing dormer window. Plain wool blanket. Plain pine furniture. A small framed sampler on the wall bearing an old Yeomanry blessing in cross-stitch.

U3 — Guest Room Two (centre)

Identical to U2. The blanket is grey-green wool. The sampler is a still-life of three apples.

U4 — Guest Room Three (east)

Identical to U2 except slightly different — the wardrobe is a touch larger, the dormer window is a touch wider. The blanket is faded blue. There is no sampler; instead, a small carved wooden cross above the bed (Heironeous mark).

This is the room Hask used to sleep in when he came home. Rosalin still makes the bed each morning. She has not said this to anyone in thirty years.

U5 — Rosalin's Chambers (full north wall)

Her bed against the north wall, a small writing desk with a chair (a worn ledger sits closed on the desk, her handwriting careful and neat across the visible page), a clothes press, a small cold hearth, a single south-facing window above the bar/kitchen below. A cedar trunk at the foot of the bed (her own things; nothing of Hask's in here). A small framed portrait on the desk — her mother, painted by a wandering artist forty years ago.

This room is not open to guests. The party should not enter it without Rosalin's permission; she will give that permission only late in the conversation, and only to one of them at a time, and only to fetch a specific object she names.

U6 — Storage Alcove (east end of corridor)

A few stacked crates, an old chest, a child's broken wooden horse on top of one crate. A small flat cedar box sits on a shelf at chest height — clearly distinguished from the other clutter by being kept clean and dust-free.

This is Hask's box. See the next page for what's in it.

Staff & routine

Rosalin runs the inn with two helping hands:

  • Tessa Brent (no blood relation despite the surname; the village she grew up in shares it widely), early twenties, kitchen and laundry. Cheerful, careful, devoted to Rosalin who took her in when her mother died four years ago. Knows nothing about Hask beyond “the mistress has a brother who travels.”
  • Old Joss, the stable-hand, late sixties, deaf in one ear, sleeps in the stable loft. Has been at the Hare for eleven years. Saw Hask twice. Says little; thinks Rosalin's brother is “a gentleman who has done well for himself,” in a tone that makes clear he means it as a sad observation, not a compliment.

The Hare opens at first bell (dawn); breakfast for any overnight guests is bread, butter, jam, and tea; midday is the busy meal (stew and bread); supper is light. Rosalin closes the kitchen at ninth bell (around 9 pm) and the common room at tenth. She is in bed by eleventh bell most nights. She rises at fourth bell to start the oven.

Costs

ServiceCost
Bed (single)1 sp
Bed (sharing)4 cp each
Stable (per horse)1 sp per night, includes oats
Breakfast2 cp
Midday meal (stew + bread)4 cp
Supper (whatever is left)3 cp
Pint of barley ale4 cp
Pint of Rosalin's amber6 cp

If the party tries to pay in gold for anything but the bed-and-stable line, Rosalin will refuse the gold and accept the equivalent in coppers and silvers. “I do not keep change for gold pieces. You are not in a city.”

Rosalin Brent — The Sister

For a portrait and stat block, see the actor Rosalin Brent in the Phase 2 NPCs compendium.

Who she is

Mid-fifties; weathered; capable. Faded auburn hair shot with grey, pulled back. Strong shoulders, hands calloused by thirty years of innkeeping. A quiet face that does not give much away on first reading but reads the room itself like other people read scripture. Grey-green eyes; a careful, guarded mouth that does not smile easily.

She has run the Sign of the Brown Hare since she was twenty-four. She owns it outright. She has never married. She does not, in 581 CY, know what to do with what she knows about her brother, which is why she has not done anything with it.

The family (GM background)

  • Mother: Ana Brent (née Wesh), a farmer's daughter from the Stell country. Died of fever in winter 549 CY, when Rosalin was 20 and Hask was 14.
  • Rosalin's father: Tomas Brent, a small-trade wool-buyer. Died on the road of a winter's chill in 537 CY, when Rosalin was 8. Rosalin remembers him in fragments — the way he laughed, the smell of his coat, the songs he sang. No portrait of him survives.
  • Hask's father: Eron Vandrell, an Aerdy-born small merchant who came west looking for work, married Ana three years after Tomas died, fathered Hask in 535, and died of a stomach illness when Hask was eleven. Hask is named after Eron's father, a Rel Astran goldsmith.
  • Rosalin and Hask: half-siblings. Same mother, different fathers. Six years apart. Rosalin was 20 when their mother died; Hask was 14. Rosalin took him in and raised him for two years until he left at sixteen, in 551 CY, to make his way east.

What Rosalin knows of the family beyond herself: she remembers Eron Vandrell as a kind, distracted, slightly tragic man who loved his wife and his son and had no idea how to make his Aerdy-trained business sense survive Yeomanry wool prices. He died poor and proud. Rosalin has never been east of Loftwick in her life.

How she has watched Hask change

Hask has come back to the Brown Hare eight times since he left in 551 CY:

YearAgeWhat Rosalin remembers
555 CY20Came back lean, sunburnt, with a small purse and a story about working as a clerk in Greyhawk City. Stayed five nights. Was, briefly, her brother again.
560 CY25Came back wearing a coat she could not have afforded. Smiled too much. Stayed three nights. Asked her if she needed anything; she said she did not.
568 CY33Came back at midwinter, very quiet, very tired. Stayed nine nights — the longest single visit since he had left as a boy. Slept badly. Did not say a word about where he had been.
572 CY37Came in well-dressed, on a hired horse, with a small leather case. Stayed two nights. Said almost nothing. Gave her a gift of a silver-handled hairbrush which she has never used.
575 CY40Came in the spring with a man she had never seen before — mid-fifties, grey-bearded, well-spoken, did not give a name. They stayed one night. They spoke for hours, alone, in the upper room. They left at dawn. This is the last visit before the Onnwal-coast incident in late 575 CY.
578 CY43Came alone, briefly. Three nights. Slept badly. Said he had been in Hardby. Did not seem like himself.
580 CY45Came at midsummer, half a day after the Hardby tavern incident the spymaster Caelith later told the party about. Stayed two nights. Was, Rosalin says, “not all there.” She thought at the time he was unwell. She believes now he had been pulled back by the contingency Caelith described.
Patchwall 580 CY (fourteen months ago)45Came late, on foot, exhausted. Stayed two nights. Slept worse than she had ever seen him sleep. Wrote a letter at her writing-desk and burned it in the kitchen hearth before he left at fourth bell. This is the last time she saw him.

What she knows operationally (very little)

  • She does not know the name “Andren.” Hask has never used it in her presence.
  • She does not know the name “Cindren & Vhal.” Hask has never spoken of the firm to her.
  • She does not know the name “Korre,” “Kethren,” or any other Greyhawk contact. She did not even know Hask had been in Greyhawk City within the last two years.
  • The man with the grey beard in 575 CY: well-spoken, Aerdy accent, dressed in dark grey wool with a small silver pin she could not identify, drank only water, slept in U4 (Hask's room) without complaint, paid the bill in clean Aerdy coin. He did not give a name. He addressed Hask as “Andren,” once, in the kitchen, when he thought Rosalin was not listening — she heard it through the door but did not understand it at the time. She remembers it now as the only operational detail she has ever heard. She will not volunteer it. She will say it if the party asks her directly, with care, about who else her brother brought to her inn.
  • The mark on Hask's map: she does not know about it. She has never seen the map. When the party shows her or describes it, her face does the same half-second thing it did at the door. “He kept us. I did not ask him to. I did not know that he had.”

How to play the conversation

Run this slowly. Rosalin will not open up at noon. She will not open up over the midday meal. She will not open up while she is working.

She will open up after the supper service is closed, after the kitchen is cleaned, after Tessa and Old Joss have gone to bed. She will pour herself a small glass of the amber ale — the only time the party will see her drink — and she will sit at the corner table by the fireplace and motion the party to sit with her. This is the scene the sidequest exists for.

She will not be questioned. She will tell the story in her own order, in her own time. If a PC tries to interrupt with a question, she will hold up a hand, finish the line she was on, and then answer.

The shape of what she says

Roughly:

  1. How she knew her brother as a boy. Bright, hot-tempered, beautiful, hungry for things he could not name. Always angry that he could not afford the coats he saw on richer boys in Stell market. The first time she caught him stealing was at twelve, an apple from a market-stall; she paid the stall-keeper and walked him home and told him she would not pay the next one.
  2. How their mother dying changed him. “He thought it was unfair. He was right. It was unfair. But he carried it as though the unfairness had been done to him personally and to no one else. I lost my mother too. I did not turn it into a sword.”
  3. How he left at sixteen. She woke one morning and his bed was empty and his small canvas pack was gone. There was a note on the kitchen table that said only Don't worry, Ros. I'll be all right. I'll write. He did write. The letters came less and less often.
  4. The coats. “The first time he came back he wore a coat that did not fit him. It was a good coat. It was somebody else's good coat. He had bought it from a man in Greyhawk, he said. He had a clerk's job, he said. I knew the coat was somebody else's and I knew the job was a lie and I said nothing because he was twenty and he was my brother and he had come home.”
  5. The man with the grey beard. “I knew when I saw the two of them at my table that I had lost something. I did not know yet what. I know now.”
  6. The thesis. The line below.

The thesis line (the line Phase 2 needs)

“Hask was never clever. He was hungry and he was beautiful and he was furious, and one of those is enough on its own to ruin a man and he had all three. Someone with a head on their shoulders found him and put work in his hands, and that work is what is wearing his face now. He is not the one writing the letters. He never could have been. Look for the hand that does the writing. That is the man who took him from me.”

Deliver this slowly. She is not performing. She has thought this for a long time. She has never said it aloud before.

If a PC asks what they can do

She will look at them for a long moment, and then say:

“If you find him — alive — you may tell him I am still here. That is enough. You do not have to bring him home. You do not have to forgive him on my behalf. You do not have to do anything except, perhaps, look at him once like you would look at a man instead of a problem, and decide for yourselves whether what you see is my brother or the man someone made of him. I think you will see the latter. If you see anything of the former — tell me. Write to me. I will not be angry to know.”

She does not, in this whole conversation, ask whether they are going to kill him. She has long since stopped asking herself that question.

Stat block

See Rosalin Brent in the Phase 2 NPCs compendium. Commoner (CR 0); HP 10; AC 10. Skills: Insight +4, Persuasion +2, Cook's Tools +5. She is not a combatant. If the party endangers her she will hide. If they protect her she will return to work the following morning as if nothing had happened.

Hask's Box & the Never-Sent Letter

Both are offered only after Rosalin has finished her conversation at the fireplace, and only if the party asks. She will not volunteer either. She will not refuse them either.

Hask's box

A small flat cedar case, about the size of a folded shirt, kept on a shelf in the upstairs storage alcove (U6). The box has been there since 555 CY, when Rosalin tidied his bedroom for the first time after his first visit home as a young man and could not bring herself to throw out the things he had left as a boy.

If the party asks to see it, she will fetch it herself. She will set it on a table. She will open it. She will not touch the contents. She will let the party look.

Contents

  1. A fishing-knife with a wooden handle. Six inches of good steel, slightly nicked at the tip, well-oiled. The handle bears two crudely-burned initials: E. V. — Eron Vandrell, Hask's father, who gave the knife to him on his eighth birthday. Hask used it for trout in the Stellwater. It is worth maybe 5 sp. It is, in any reasonable sense, priceless.
  2. A child's prayer-book to Heironeous. Thumbed at twelve, the binding loose, certain pages marked with small dog-ears. The frontispiece is a child's hand-lettered name: Hask, eleven years. The dog-eared pages are: the morning prayer, the prayer before a meal, the prayer for a parent who is ill (heavily annotated, in a child's hand, with the words FATHER, MOTHER, and once — in a different ink, perhaps three years later — just ROS).
  3. A small wooden horse. Carved from a soft pale wood (pine, probably), about the size of a fist, the head broken off and clumsily re-glued at some point. Investigation DC 8 reads the carving as a child's first attempt at woodwork — the proportions are wrong, the legs are too short, but the attempt is careful and serious. The break is old; the glue is older.
  4. One folded letter — SEE BELOW. (Rosalin does not include this in the box's contents when she opens it; the letter is kept separately in a kitchen drawer.)

None of the box's contents are evidence. None of them are conspiracy artifacts. None of them carry the “E.” hand or the crane-and-coins seal or any other operational marker. They are a boy's things, kept by his sister, in the room he used to sleep in.

Rosalin will not let the party take any of these things away. She will let them hold each item, look at it, and put it back. “These are not yours. They are not mine either. They are his. If he ever comes home they will be here.”

The never-sent letter

This is the part of the sidequest that may matter in Phase 6.

If the party asks, in the conversation at the fireplace or later in the morning, whether there is anything they can carry to Hask if they find him — or if they offer, of their own initiative, to do that — Rosalin will pause for a long count, walk to the kitchen, open a drawer beside the oven, and remove a flat oilcloth-wrapped packet. She brings it back to the table.

Inside the oilcloth: a single folded sheet of plain Yeomanry paper, sealed with a paste of flour and water (a child's seal — a bread-seal — she could not bring herself to use proper wax). The fold is creased softly from being kept folded for years. The seal is unbroken.

On the outside, in Rosalin's careful handwriting: Hask.

What she says when she gives it

“I wrote this five winters ago. I never sent it because I did not have an address to send it to and I was not sure I wanted him to read it when he was already a man I did not know any more. If you find him in a room you leave alive — alive — you may put this on the table in front of him. You will not say my name. You will not tell him you came south. You will leave it there and you will go. He will read it or he will not. That is his to do.”

She does not say what is in the letter.

What is in the letter (GM-only)

If the party reads the letter at any point — which is a small betrayal Rosalin will not catch but which will sit on the PC who breaks the seal — the letter says, in her tidy careful hand:

Hask —

I have been thinking about the morning you left. I did not understand it then. I think I understand it now.

You were sixteen and you thought you were leaving for the east. I think you were leaving from this room because you could not bear the way it looked at fourteen with no mother in it, and you could not bear the way it looked at sixteen with only me in it, and you could not bear the way it would look at eighteen when I would still be there and you would still be poor.

I am not angry about the leaving. I am not even angry about the years after. I am angry that you let someone use you. I am angry that you let it happen so easy. I would have told you, if you had come home a single time and asked me, that whatever they were offering was not worth your name. You did not ask me. You wore other people's coats and you came home looking less like Hask each time and you let yourself be made smaller. That is the thing I cannot forgive you for. The rest I have already forgiven.

Come home if you can. The bed is made. The horse is old but the road is fine. We will not talk about the years. We will talk about what to plant in the spring.

— Ros

How the letter pays off in Phase 6 (carry-forward)

If the party has the letter when they reach the Brass Crow in Phase 6 and recover Haskur Vandrell alive — whether they take him into custody, persuade him to talk, or simply have him at the end of an interview — the PC who carries the letter has the option to set it on a table in front of him.

If they do:

  • Haskur will recognise the handwriting before he reads a word. His face will do the same half-second thing his sister's did at the door of the Brown Hare. He will not speak for a long count.
  • He will read the letter. He will not let the party see his face.
  • He will, once, ask the PC who put the letter down: “Is she well?” Answer however is honest.
  • Whatever the party then asks him — about Korre, about Andren, about the firm, about “E.” — he will answer the next two questions truthfully. Not three. Not four. Two. After that he will close again.
  • He will not, under any circumstance, accept the letter to keep. He will fold it again, set it back on the table, and push it toward the PC. “Take it back to her. Tell her you brought it. I do not need it. I have it now.”

The letter is, in mechanical terms, two truthful answers from Haskur Vandrell at the Brass Crow. The party may spend them on anything they like. If they ask him to confirm Korre's identity, he will. If they ask whether Sereth knows him, he will say yes. If they ask who “E.” is — the campaign's central question — he will say “I do not know. I have never been told. I do not think the man I work for knows either. The hand comes from above us.” Which is true.

This is the sidequest's only operational payoff and it arrives in Phase 6, not Phase 2.

If the party does NOT take the letter

Rosalin will not be hurt. She will put the oilcloth back in the drawer. She will say, “That is fine. I have kept it five winters; I can keep it more.” She will pour them another cup of tea. There is no narrative penalty for declining. The letter remains in the drawer at the Brown Hare for the rest of the campaign.

If the party reads the letter and then re-seals it

Rosalin will not know. The seal is bread-and-water paste and any PC with Sleight of Hand DC 10 can re-form it convincingly. However: at the Brass Crow scene, if Haskur reads it, he will pause halfway through and say, quietly, “Someone has read this before me.” He will finish reading it. He will still answer two questions truthfully. But his last line will not be “take it back to her.” It will be: “She would not have wanted you to read it. Do not tell her.” And he is right.

The Ride Back — Days 4 and 5

Departure from the Brown Hare

The party leaves the Hare in the late afternoon of the third day. The light is thinning. The wash-line has been brought in. Tessa is in the kitchen kneading dough for the morning. Old Joss is in the stable with Burdock.

Read-aloud at the gate

Rosalin sees you off at the front of the inn. She does not stand long in the road. She does not wave. She nods once, the small careful nod of a country woman who has seen people leave her door for thirty years.

“Ride well,” she says. “The road forks at the second milestone. Take the left and you are back on the Wool by sundown.”

And then, because she is thinking it and does not see the use in hiding it:

“If I do not see you again — thank you for the company. The common room has not been six strangers and a country woman around a fire in a long time. I will sit at that table tomorrow night and have my cup of amber and remember it. You did me a kindness without meaning to.”

She turns and goes back inside before any of you can answer.

The door closes. The lamp inside the door comes on.

You ride east toward the milestone.

The night at the Plough

The party sleeps at Stell on Day 4. Mistress Cosk does not ask where they have been. She gives them the same room (or two rooms) they used on the way down. The lamb stew is as good as the first time. One of the PCs — whichever is most thoughtful — finds themselves making conversation with Mistress Cosk about whether she remembers a Rosalin Brent from the southern road. Maela does not. She knows the Hare; she does not know the woman who runs it. “She does not come up to Stell. I do not know if she has come up to Stell in twenty years.”

This is, for any PC who is paying attention, the last small detail of how alone Rosalin is.

Pressure Clock advances

End of dayTriggerAdvance
Day 4Korre's watcher in Loftwick has lost the party. He reports up. Cindren & Vhal Hardby is informed that the “eastern delegation” appears to have ridden out of town and is unaccounted-for.+1 segment
Day 5The party rides back into Loftwick at midday. The watcher reacquires them at the South Gate. Broadsheet rumour is starting to swirl in the city — an anonymous note has appeared at the Folio of the Mere asking the questions Caelith has been asking quietly. Pressure Clock segment 3 (public rumour) fires if it hadn't already.+1 segment (and segment-3 effects, if applicable)

If the party departed Loftwick at clock 2 (the typical pre-Moll state), they return at clock 4 — the “witness loss” segment. The GM should consult the Pressure Clock segment table in Phase 2 — Quick Reference and resolve one witness loss before the party can pick up their work where they left off. Pollow is the recommended cut here — he was the most exposed witness and the most vulnerable to the firm's response time.

Return read-aloud

You ride into Loftwick at midday on the fifth day. The Civic Way is busy with mid-day foot traffic. The same militia footmen who escorted you in from the north a week ago are not on the South Gate; you do not know whichever pair is. They do not stop you. They do glance at each other.

You ride down the Civic Way toward the Little Palace. There is a small printed broadsheet sheet nailed to a notice-post that you did not see when you left. The headline reads, in poor cheap print: What does the Audit Hall keep in its little palace these days, and who is foreign in this question?

A clerk in a black coat passes you and does not meet your eyes.

Caelith is waiting at the gate. He looks at you in a way that says he knows where you have been. He does not ask. He does not need to.

“Welcome back,” he says. “The matter is not where you left it. Come inside. Some news is for the table; some is for the cellar.”

Caelith's debrief (truncated)

Once inside, Caelith tells the party what they have lost during their absence. The GM should choose whichever Pressure Clock segment fires; the most common is:

  • Pollow has been found dead at his Wick Lane office, certified by another contract physician (not the watch). The party loses Pollow as a witness. Caelith, on hearing the party's account of where they were, says only:

“I should have moved Pollow sooner. I will remember.”

He does not ask what they found at the Brown Hare. If a PC volunteers it — “Haskur has a sister” — he will pause for a long count, and then say, very quietly:

“I am glad to hear it. He had a face. He had a room he slept in. He had a sister. I have been carrying him in my files for nine years without ever quite believing in any of those things. Thank you for telling me.”

That is the entirety of his response. He will not write to Rosalin. He will not put her name in any file. He will simply hold the fact, the way he holds many quiet facts.

What the sidequest leaves the party with

  • The thesis — Caelith's framing of Haskur as the man someone made, delivered now in a sister's grief. The party leaves Phase 2 with this conviction held in two registers, not one.
  • The never-sent letter (if they took it) — carrying-forward as two truthful answers from Haskur Vandrell at the Brass Crow in Phase 6. A scene-shape, not a clue.
  • One PC, marked. Whichever PC carried the letter, or the boy's wooden horse in their head, or Rosalin's thesis in their ears, will have a small private edge in any future scene that involves Haskur Vandrell as a person rather than as an antagonist. The GM should give them advantage on the first Insight or Persuasion check against Haskur in any later phase, with the explanation, in fiction, that they keep almost calling him Hask.
  • A clock cost they paid willingly. The party rode south knowing it would cost them. They came back knowing what they had lost. That is its own small form of integrity, and the campaign should honour it.

Carrying Rosalin forward

Rosalin does not appear in any other Phase 2–9 journal. That is deliberate. She is at the Brown Hare. She is fine. She is not in danger. The conspiracy does not know about her and the GM should not put it on the table by giving her one. If the party asks Caelith, in a later phase, to send word to her about Haskur's eventual fate — the GM should grant the request. Caelith will send word. Rosalin will read the news at her writing-desk in late winter, and she will sit at the corner table by the fireplace afterward, and she will pour herself a small glass of the amber ale, and she will be alright.

That is enough. Do not write any other scene with her unless the table specifically asks. The sidequest is over. The relief beat has landed.
